title: A2A (Agent-to-Agent Protocol)
tldr: Open HTTP protocol for agents to discover, authenticate, and hand off tasks across vendors.
plain_english: A2A is how one AI agent talks to another AI agent from a different company or system—like calling a colleague in another department. Instead of building custom bridges between every pair of agents, A2A publishes a shared rulebook: here's what I can do, here's how to reach me, here's how to prove you're trusted. Think of it as a standardized business card and handshake for agents.
how_it_works: Each A2A-enabled agent publishes an agent card—a JSON file listing its capabilities, authentication requirements, and available endpoints. When one agent needs work done (e.g., "send this invoice to billing"), it queries the discovery registry, finds a compatible agent, authenticates via the card's spec, and sends a structured task message. The receiving agent processes it, returns a result, and both log the interaction. Security is baked in: signatures, token validation, and optional encryption.
why_it_matters: Cross-vendor agent networks only work if agents speak the same language. A2A removes the engineering tax of custom integrations—instead of writing code to bridge Salesforce-to-SAP-to-Stripe agents, you plug them in like modules. Early adoption by Google, Salesforce, and SAP signals vendor commitment, but production deployments are still sparse.
example: A customer service agent at Company A receives a refund request. Instead of a human forwarding the ticket, the agent queries A2A, discovers Company B's billing agent, authenticates via the published card, and sends a structured refund task. Company B's agent processes it, updates records, and sends confirmation back—all without custom middleware or hardcoded endpoints.
pitfalls: Confusing A2A with MCP: A2A is agent-to-agent; MCP is agent-to-tool. They solve different problems and often run together.
Assuming A2A handles all security automatically—you still own threat modeling, token rotation, and network access controls.
Publishing an agent card without versioning; breaking changes in capabilities or auth schemas will break downstream agents silently.
Treating A2A as a replacement for API gateways; it doesn't load-balance, rate-limit, or monitor at scale yet.
when_use: Use A2A when you're orchestrating agents from multiple vendors or teams and want to avoid custom point-to-point integrations.
when_avoid: Avoid A2A for single-vendor, closed-loop workflows or when agents live on the same infrastructure and can share memory directly.

faq.q: What's the difference between A2A and MCP?
faq.a: MCP is agent-to-tool: an agent calls external tools (APIs, databases, files). A2A is agent-to-agent: one agent delegates work to another agent. They're complementary—an A2A agent often uses MCP internally to call tools.

faq.q: Is A2A production-ready?
faq.a: Early adopters (Google, Salesforce, SAP) are testing it, but widespread production deployments are not yet common. Expect 2025–2026 for stable tooling and standardized implementations.

faq.q: How does A2A handle security and trust between agents?
faq.a: Agents publish cryptographic signatures, API keys, or OAuth tokens in their agent cards. The calling agent verifies identity before sending tasks. You're still responsible for rotating credentials, vetting agent publishers, and monitoring for abuse.

faq.q: Do I need to rewrite my agents to support A2A?
faq.a: Not necessarily. You can wrap existing agents with an A2A adapter layer that translates between A2A messages and your agent's internal format.

faq.q: What happens if an A2A agent goes offline or fails?
faq.a: A2A doesn't handle retries or fallbacks natively—that's your orchestration layer's job. You'll need to implement circuit breakers, timeouts, and redundant agent discovery.

title: Admin API (OpenAI / Anthropic)
tldr: Management API that controls API keys, teams, budgets, and usage metrics—separate from inference APIs.
plain_english: Think of it like the control panel for your LLM infrastructure. While your inference API is what your applications actually call to generate text, the Admin API is where you manage who has access, how much they can spend, and what they're actually using. It's the operations side, not the execution side.
how_it_works: You authenticate with an admin key that has elevated permissions. Through REST endpoints, you can programmatically create and rotate API keys, set per-project spending limits, invite team members with specific roles, fetch usage reports, and configure rate limits. Each LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) exposes its own Admin API with different endpoint structures and features. Changes take effect immediately—you don't need to redeploy applications.
why_it_matters: In teams with multiple projects or dozens of developers, manual key management becomes a security and cost nightmare. Admin APIs let you enforce spend guardrails automatically, audit who called what and when, and revoke access instantly if a key leaks. This is where enterprise governance lives.
example: A fintech startup with 15 developers uses OpenAI's Admin API to create one API key per developer, each with a $500/month budget. When a junior engineer's key is accidentally committed to GitHub, they rotate it through the API in 30 seconds without waiting for ops. Their billing dashboard auto-refreshes with usage per team, showing that the analytics team burned through $8K last month while the chatbot team spent $2K.
pitfalls: Treating the admin key the same as a regular API key—if compromised, an attacker can delete all your keys, drain budgets, and invite themselves as a team member.
Assuming all providers' Admin APIs work the same—Anthropic's Workbench is not a full API replacement; OpenAI's model and endpoint coverage differ from Anthropic's.
Setting budgets and forgetting to monitor them—limits prevent runaway costs but won't alert you before you hit them; pair with observability tools.
Not rotating keys regularly—a leaked admin key found in logs three months later can be exploited retroactively.
when_use: Use the Admin API when you have multiple teams, need automated provisioning, want to track costs per project, or require access revocation without application restarts.
when_avoid: Avoid it for single-developer projects without compliance requirements, or if your LLM provider only offers it via dashboard (no API alternative).

faq.q: What's the difference between an Admin API key and a regular API key?
faq.a: A regular key can only call inference endpoints (chat completions, embeddings). An admin key can create/revoke keys, modify budgets, manage teams, and fetch usage metrics. Never expose an admin key to your applications.

faq.q: Can I use the Admin API to set a per-user spending limit?
faq.a: OpenAI supports per-API-key limits; you create one key per user and set a budget on it. Anthropic's approach differs—check their current documentation. Most providers don't offer true per-user limits across all keys owned by one account.

faq.q: How quickly do Admin API changes take effect?
faq.a: Rate limit and budget changes are usually immediate (within seconds). Key creation is instant, but revocation may take a few seconds to propagate across all inference servers.

faq.q: Do I need the Admin API if I'm using a single API key?
faq.a: No. If one key is shared across your whole team and you don't need programmatic access control or cost tracking, the dashboard UI alone is enough.

faq.q: What happens if I hit my Admin API rate limit?
faq.a: You'll get a 429 response. Most providers allow hundreds of admin calls per minute, but if you're provisioning thousands of keys daily, you may need to batch requests or contact support.

title: Agent Card
tldr: JSON document published at /.well-known/agent-card.json that advertises an agent's capabilities, auth, and endpoints.
plain_english: An agent card is like a resume for AI agents — a standardized file that tells other systems what the agent can do, how to authenticate with it, and where to send requests. You publish it at a fixed location (similar to how robots.txt works for web crawlers), so any A2A-compatible client can discover and interact with your agent without custom integration.
how_it_works: Your agent serves a JSON document at /.well-known/agent-card.json containing: agent name, supported skills, content modalities (text, image, etc.), authentication scheme (API key, OAuth), A2A endpoint URLs, and whether it supports streaming. A client fetches this card, parses the auth requirements and available skills, performs authentication if needed, then makes A2A protocol calls to the advertised endpoints. The card acts as the handshake layer — it answers 'Can I talk to this agent?' and 'How do I do it?' before any actual work begins.
why_it_matters: Agent cards enable automatic discovery and interoperability in multi-agent systems. Without them, each agent integration requires custom documentation and code. With agent cards, clients can programmatically verify compatibility, fetch auth details, and route requests correctly — reducing friction when composing agents from different sources and teams.
example: A customer-support team publishes an agent card for their refund-handling agent at api.support.internal/.well-known/agent-card.json. The card advertises two skills: 'process-refund' and 'check-order-status', requires API key auth, and points to the A2A endpoint at /v1/agent. A fulfillment agent from inventory needs help with a customer issue, fetches the card, validates the auth header format, then calls the refund-handler directly using the endpoint URL and skill list.
pitfalls: Publishing stale endpoint URLs or skill lists in the card — clients will fail silently or route to non-existent handlers.
Forgetting to include all auth schemes; clients may attempt auth methods you don't support, then give up rather than falling back to other options.
Using vague skill names or missing required metadata (modalities, response streaming); consumers can't determine if the agent fits their use case.
Placing the card at a non-standard location or under authentication — discovery becomes impossible if the card itself requires auth.
when_use: Use agent cards when you're building A2A-compatible services, need other agents or clients to discover your capabilities automatically, or want to reduce custom integration logic in multi-agent orchestration.
when_avoid: Avoid if your agent is internal-only and manually configured, or if your system uses a central registry instead of distributed discovery via well-known URLs.

faq.q: What's the difference between an agent card and an OpenAPI spec?
faq.a: An agent card is A2A-specific metadata: it advertises agent capabilities and auth for peer-to-peer discovery. OpenAPI describes HTTP APIs in general. Agent cards often reference an OpenAPI spec for endpoint details, but the card itself is lighter and agent-focused.

faq.q: Do I need an agent card if I'm using a centralized agent registry?
faq.a: No. Agent cards enable distributed discovery. If your system has a central registry that clients query instead, you can register there without publishing a card. Use the card only if you want agents to be discoverable at a standard URL.

faq.q: Can an agent card change without breaking existing clients?
faq.a: Yes, if you add new skills or auth methods while keeping existing ones stable. But removing skills or changing endpoint URLs will break clients that cached the card. Version your changes or maintain backwards compatibility.

faq.q: What happens if a client can't fetch the agent card?
faq.a: The client cannot discover your agent's capabilities, auth, or endpoints. It will either fail or fall back to manual configuration. Always ensure your card is publicly accessible at the standard location.

faq.q: Is the agent card the same as a model card?
faq.a: No. A model card describes an AI model's training, capabilities, and biases. An agent card describes an agent service's skills, endpoints, and auth. They serve different purposes.

title: Anthropic API Prompt Caching
tldr: Mark Claude API content blocks with cache_control to reuse prefixes, cutting token costs by 90% after a 25% write premium.
plain_english: Think of prompt caching like memoizing a function call. You tell Claude's API "remember this system prompt and context" once, pay a small upfront cost, then reuse it on follow-up requests for a massive discount. It's especially valuable when you're sending the same large document, code, or instructions repeatedly—each new query reuses the cached part instead of re-processing it.
how_it_works: Add a `cache_control` field with `type: 'ephemeral'` to any message block (system, user, or tool definitions). On the first call, you pay 25% extra per cached token as a write cost. Every subsequent request that shares the same prefix uses the cache: those tokens cost 90% less. The cache lives for 5 minutes by default; you can opt into 1-hour TTL. If the prefix changes, the cache miss triggers a new write cycle.
why_it_matters: For agents, RAG systems, and multi-turn workflows with stable context (e.g., large documents or code libraries), prompt caching often cuts API spend in half. It's a direct lever on cost per query without sacrificing latency or quality.
example: A legal tech startup processes contract reviews where each request includes a 50KB legal standard and analysis framework. Without caching, every call repays for those 50K tokens. With caching, they pay 25% extra on the first request, then 90% discount on the next 99 queries—amortizing the cost across the batch. Their per-token cost drops from $0.003 to ~$0.0003 for cached tokens.
pitfalls: Cache hits only occur if the prefix exactly matches; a single system prompt change voids the cache and triggers a new write cycle.
The 5-minute default TTL is aggressive for batch jobs; you must explicitly enable 1-hour caching or revert to paying write premiums for each batch.
Caching applies to the entire prefix, not individual blocks; you cannot selectively cache only the system prompt if the user message varies.
The 25% write premium means small, frequently-invalidated prefixes cost more than they save; use only for stable, reused context.
when_use: Use prompt caching when you have large stable prefixes (e.g., documents, code, system instructions) reused across multiple requests, especially in agent loops, RAG, or batch inference.
when_avoid: Avoid caching when prefixes change frequently, context is small (<1KB), or you process each request once; the write premium and complexity overhead outweigh savings.

faq.q: How much do I save with Anthropic prompt caching?
faq.a: After paying a 25% write premium on first use, cached tokens cost 90% less on subsequent requests. For a 50KB context reused 10 times, you amortize the premium and achieve ~6–8x lower per-token cost overall.

faq.q: What happens if my prompt changes slightly?
faq.a: Any change to the cached prefix invalidates the entire cache and triggers a new write cycle at 25% premium. Exact prefix matching is required; plan stable context accordingly.

faq.q: Can I use prompt caching with extended thinking?
faq.a: Yes, but cache control applies only to input tokens, not reasoning tokens generated during extended thinking. The discount applies to reused context, not to the thinking process itself.

faq.q: How long does a cache stay active?
faq.a: By default, 5 minutes. You can opt into a 1-hour TTL via the API. After expiration, the next request with the same prefix pays the 25% write premium again.

faq.q: Is prompt caching available on all Claude models?
faq.a: Prompt caching is available on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Opus, and newer versions. Check Anthropic's API docs for the most current support.

title: API
tldr: A standardized way to send requests to a model and receive responses programmatically over HTTP.
plain_english: An API is a contract between your code and a service—you make a structured request (usually JSON), the service processes it, and returns a structured response. In AI, you send text or instructions to a model endpoint, the model runs inference, and you get back generated text or tokens. Most AI APIs follow a simple pattern: one endpoint receives your message, another returns the result.
how_it_works: You send an HTTP POST request containing a JSON payload with your prompt and parameters (temperature, max tokens, etc.) to a model's endpoint. The server receives your request, runs the model's forward pass (inference), and returns a JSON response with the generated text and metadata (tokens used, finish reason). Many tools—Ollama, LM Studio, LocalAI, and most commercial services—expose OpenAI-compatible APIs, so the same code works across different models and providers without rewriting.
why_it_matters: APIs are the boundary between using AI in a GUI and building AI into production systems. Once you can make API calls, you integrate models into applications, automate workflows at scale, and swap providers or models without rewriting application logic.
example: A customer-support team uses LangChain to build a chatbot. They make API calls to OpenAI's Chat Completions endpoint with customer messages. OpenAI returns a response in < 500ms. The same code later points to a local Ollama instance running Llama for cost reduction—only the API endpoint URL changes, not the application code.
pitfalls: Treating all API parameters the same: temperature 0.7 works for brainstorming but fails for deterministic classification—adjust for the task.
Not handling rate limits or retries: production APIs fail; your code must back off exponentially and retry transient errors.
Ignoring token cost per request: a 10k-token response at scale drains budgets fast—measure and set max_tokens bounds.
Assuming API response time is fixed: cold model boots, queue depth, and concurrent load all affect latency; test under production load.
when_use: Use APIs when you need to integrate models into applications, distribute inference across a network, or swap models without rebuilding code.
when_avoid: Avoid APIs for local development or privacy-critical work where latency or data residency is non-negotiable—run models locally instead.

faq.q: What is an API in AI?
faq.a: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a programmatic way to send text to a model and receive generated output. You make HTTP requests to a model endpoint; it returns JSON with the result.

faq.q: Is OpenAI's API the only standard?
faq.a: No. OpenAI's Chat Completions API became the de facto standard, so Ollama, LM Studio, and LocalAI implement OpenAI-compatible APIs. But other formats (Claude's Messages API, Anthropic's Workbench) also exist.

faq.q: How much does an API call cost?
faq.a: Cost depends on the provider and model. OpenAI charges per token (input and output); Ollama is free (self-hosted). Always check pricing and multiply by your expected token volume.

faq.q: Can I use the same API code for different models?
faq.a: Yes, if both models expose OpenAI-compatible APIs. You change the endpoint URL and model name; the request/response format stays the same. Custom models may need adapter code.

faq.q: What happens if an API request fails?
faq.a: The server returns an HTTP error code (429 for rate limit, 500 for server error) and a JSON error message. Your code should retry transient errors with exponential backoff and handle permanent errors gracefully.

title: Assistants API (OpenAI)
tldr: OpenAI's stateful API for building conversational assistants with persistent threads, file handling, and built-in tools.
plain_english: Think of Assistants API as a mailbox system for your AI agent. Instead of stateless request-response calls, you create a persistent thread (conversation) that remembers every message, file, and tool call. The API handles the scaffolding—message history, file attachments, code execution—so you focus on the conversation logic rather than state management.
how_it_works: You create an Assistant (with instructions and tool definitions), then create a Thread for each user conversation. Messages are posted to the thread; the Assistant processes them sequentially, calling tools (code interpreter, file search, function calls) as needed. The API maintains conversation context across requests, returning message deltas and tool results. Unlike Chat Completions, you don't reconstruct the full conversation each call—the thread does that for you.
why_it_matters: Most 2023–2024 production systems for document analysis, code debugging, and multi-turn agentic workflows used Assistants because it reduced plumbing code. Understanding it matters for maintaining existing deployments, even as OpenAI steers new work toward Responses API.
example: A customer support team builds an assistant that reads uploaded PDFs, searches their content, and executes Python scripts to validate claims. Using Assistants API, each support ticket becomes a thread; context persists across replies without manual state wrangling. Compare that to Chat Completions, where you'd need to re-send the entire conversation history and manage file references yourself on every turn.
pitfalls: Threads and runs are separate entities; forgetting to poll run status or check for failures before reading messages leads to race conditions.
File search (Retrieval tool) indexes files at Assistant creation time, not upload time—stale documents persist until you rebuild the Assistant.
Token usage bills for the entire thread context on each run, not just your latest message; long conversations become expensive without archival.
Assistants API is being deprecated in favor of Responses API; new projects should evaluate migration costs early.
when_use: Use Assistants API when building multi-turn conversational workflows with persistent state, file uploads, or tool calls across a user session, and you're maintaining existing 2023–2024 code.
when_avoid: Avoid if starting a new project—use Responses API instead. Also avoid for stateless, single-turn queries or when you need fine-grained control over message formatting.

faq.q: What's the difference between Assistants API and Chat Completions API?
faq.a: Chat Completions is stateless: you send a full message array each call. Assistants API is stateful: threads persist conversation history server-side, and the API manages tools and file references automatically. Assistants trades request-by-request control for less boilerplate.

faq.q: Is Assistants API being replaced?
faq.a: Yes. OpenAI is deprecating Assistants API in favor of Responses API (released late 2024). If starting new work, use Responses. If maintaining existing Assistants code, understand the migration path but don't rewrite unless you hit performance or cost cliffs.

faq.q: How do I handle files in Assistants?
faq.a: Upload files to the API, attach them to threads or messages, then enable the file search or code interpreter tool. The Assistant accesses them during execution. Be aware: file search indexes at Assistant creation, not upload—changes require rebuilding.

faq.q: Why is my Assistant call timing out?
faq.a: Runs can take seconds to minutes, especially with file search or code execution. You must poll the run status endpoint (not wait synchronously). If polling returns failure, check the run's error message—common causes are malformed function calls or resource limits.

title: Batch API
tldr: Async LLM API that processes thousands of requests offline within 24 hours at ~50% cost savings.
plain_english: Instead of sending requests one at a time and waiting for answers, you upload a file of requests to a Batch API, walk away, and come back hours or days later with all results. It's like dropping off dry cleaning in the morning instead of waiting for each shirt to be cleaned individually. You trade speed for savings—half the price or better.
how_it_works: You format requests as JSONL (one JSON object per line) containing chat completion or message payloads, upload the file via the API, and receive a batch ID. The provider queues and processes your requests asynchronously, often during off-peak hours. Once finished (typically within 24 hours), you retrieve results from the same batch ID. Cost drops because providers schedule your work around their spare capacity rather than reserving real-time infrastructure for you.
why_it_matters: Dataset labeling, synthetic data generation, and large-scale evaluations consume enormous LLM tokens. Batch APIs cut those bills in half while handling scale effortlessly—thousands to millions of requests in a single file. For offline workflows, this is the default cost optimization.
example: A data team at a fintech company labels 50,000 customer support tickets for intent classification. Instead of calling the API 50,000 times over days (high latency and cost), they upload a JSONL file with all 50,000 requests to OpenAI Batch at 9 PM. By morning, results are ready. Cost: ~$25 instead of $50.
pitfalls: No real-time feedback—you cannot adjust or cancel requests mid-batch; plan file contents carefully before submission.
Fixed 24-hour SLA window; use only for truly offline workloads, not anything user-facing or time-critical.
JSONL syntax errors in even one request can corrupt parsing; validate the entire file locally before upload.
Rate limits per batch file (typically millions of tokens) are high but not infinite; monitor cumulative costs across multiple batches.
when_use: Use Batch APIs for offline evaluation, dataset labeling, synthetic data generation, or any large-scale batch job where you can tolerate 24-hour latency.
when_avoid: Avoid for user-facing workloads, real-time chat, or any scenario where response latency under seconds is a requirement.

faq.q: How much does Batch API cost compared to regular API?
faq.a: Batch APIs cost roughly 50% less than synchronous calls on the same model. Exact discounts vary by provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) but typically range 40–60%.

faq.q: Can I cancel a batch once I've submitted it?
faq.a: Most providers allow cancellation within minutes of submission, but once processing starts, you cannot recall individual requests. Design your batch carefully before upload.

faq.q: What's the difference between Batch API and continuous batching?
faq.a: Batch API is client-initiated; you upload a file and wait hours. Continuous batching is server-side; the provider automatically groups real-time requests behind the scenes for efficiency.

faq.q: Does Batch API work with vision models or just text?
faq.a: Support varies by provider. OpenAI Batch supports text and vision; check your provider's current documentation for multimodal support.

faq.q: What happens if my batch file has errors?
faq.a: The entire batch or specific requests may fail. Invalid JSONL syntax, missing required fields, or unsupported model names will cause errors. Validate your file locally before submission.

title: Chat Completions API
tldr: API standard for chat-based LLM calls: send message history, get assistant response back.
plain_english: Think of it like texting a friend—you send a list of messages (who said what, in order), and the LLM responds with one new message. The API wraps that conversation in a structured format: each message has a role (system, user, assistant, or tool) and content. You control how the model responds with settings like temperature and max_tokens.
how_it_works: You send an array of message objects, each with a role and content field. The system role sets context; user and assistant roles form the conversation history. The API processes this history, generates a completion (or streams it token-by-token), and returns a new assistant message. You can control randomness (temperature), length (max_tokens), word penalties, stop sequences, and even tool definitions. Most inference providers—OpenAI, Anthropic, Together, vLLM—implement this same contract.
why_it_matters: Chat Completions became the de facto LLM API standard because it maps directly to how people use LLMs: conversational back-and-forth. Every framework, library, and commercial service expects this format. Using it means your code works across multiple providers with minimal changes—swap an API key, keep the same interface.
example: A customer support team uses OpenAI's Chat Completions API to build a chatbot. They send a system message defining the bot's persona, then the user's latest question, then call the API. OpenAI returns an assistant response. When they decide to reduce costs, they switch the endpoint URL to a Mistral-compatible server but leave the request/response code unchanged.
pitfalls: Forgetting to include system messages: they set model behavior but are often omitted, causing inconsistent tone or off-topic responses.
Confusing role='assistant' in history with the returned completion—the API returns one new assistant message, not a choice among history.
Not setting max_tokens on length-sensitive requests, causing unexpected overage charges or truncation.
Assuming tool/function definitions work identically across providers—each interprets them slightly differently.
when_use: Use Chat Completions when building conversational interfaces, multi-turn chatbots, or any workflow where you maintain message history and need structured, role-tagged responses.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need true streaming of non-text modalities, require deterministic behavior, or are calling a provider that doesn't support this format (rare, but some domain-specific APIs use custom contracts).

faq.q: What's the difference between Chat Completions and the Completions API?
faq.a: Completions takes raw text input and continues it; Chat Completions takes structured conversation history (roles + messages) and is built for multi-turn dialogue. Chat Completions is the modern standard.

faq.q: Do I have to include the entire message history every time I call the API?
faq.a: Yes—the API is stateless. You send the full conversation context each call. Most frameworks cache this to reduce tokens, but the API itself doesn't remember previous requests.

faq.q: Can I use Chat Completions with open-source models?
faq.a: Yes. vLLM, Ollama, Replicate, and self-hosted inference servers expose OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions endpoints. You swap the base URL and keep your request code identical.

faq.q: What does the 'tool' role do?
faq.a: Tool role messages contain the output of function calls you made. After the model requests a function, you call it, then send the result back in a tool message so the model can continue reasoning.

faq.q: Is temperature the only way to control randomness?
faq.a: No. You can also use top_p (nucleus sampling), top_k, min_p, or frequency_penalty. Temperature scales all logits uniformly; top_p keeps only the smallest set of tokens summing to probability p.

title: Embeddings API
tldr: API endpoint that converts text or images to dense vectors for semantic search, retrieval, and classification tasks.
plain_english: An embeddings API takes your text (or images) and converts it into a list of numbers—a vector—that represents its meaning mathematically. Think of it like a translation layer: on one side you feed words, on the other you get coordinates in vector space where similar ideas cluster nearby. This lets you compare documents by meaning rather than exact keyword matches.
how_it_works: You send text or image content to an API endpoint (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere Embed v3, Google text-embedding). The model encodes your input into a fixed-size vector (typically 256–3,072 dimensions). Most APIs accept batches of hundreds or thousands of items per request, support dimension reduction via truncation, and let you specify whether the input is a search query or a document to retrieve. You get back vectors you store in a vector database or compare directly.
why_it_matters: Embeddings APIs are the foundational component for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), semantic search, and clustering at scale on managed platforms. Without them, you either build your own embedding model (expensive) or use keyword search (brittle). They let you ship semantic intelligence without maintaining infrastructure.
example: A customer support team uses an embeddings API to index 50,000 historical support tickets. When a new ticket arrives, they embed the customer's question and search their indexed tickets for the most similar ones. The API converts both the question and historical tickets to vectors; cosine similarity finds the best matches in milliseconds—no keyword tuning needed.
pitfalls: Confusing query embeddings with document embeddings: some models (e.g., BGE, Cohere) train separate embeddings for queries vs. documents; using the wrong type tanks retrieval quality.
Ignoring dimension truncation costs: requesting full 3,072-dim vectors when 256 dims suffice adds latency and storage overhead without accuracy gains.
Not batching requests: calling the API once per item instead of batching thousands incurs per-request overhead and hits rate limits faster.

Assuming all embeddings are interchangeable: different models (OpenAI vs. Cohere vs. Voyage) produce vectors in different spaces; switching models mid-production requires re-embedding all stored data.
when_use: Use embeddings APIs when you need semantic search, clustering, or classification on text/image data and don't want to maintain your own embedding model infrastructure.
when_avoid: Avoid embeddings APIs if you need strict latency guarantees under 50ms, operate in an offline environment, or require embeddings for languages/domains where the API model hasn't been trained.

faq.q: What is the difference between embeddings API and embedding model?
faq.a: An embedding model is the neural network that does the encoding. An embeddings API is the hosted service exposing that model. You call the API; it runs the model and returns vectors. Most practitioners use APIs rather than self-hosting because it eliminates infrastructure burden.

faq.q: How much does it cost to embed data with an embeddings API?
faq.a: Pricing varies: OpenAI charges per million input tokens (e.g., $0.02/M for text-embedding-3-small), Cohere charges per million input tokens (cheaper for bulk), and some open-source models via inference-as-a-service are cheaper but require vendor hosting. Batch operations often qualify for volume discounts.

faq.q: Can I use one embeddings API to search data embedded by another?
faq.a: No. Embeddings from different models live in different vector spaces. OpenAI and Cohere embeddings are not comparable directly. If you switch providers, you must re-embed all your existing data.

faq.q: Do embeddings APIs support images?
faq.a: Some do: OpenAI text-embedding-3 does not, but models like CLIP via hosted APIs (e.g., Replicate, Hugging Face Inference) handle images. Check the specific API's documentation; most general-purpose embeddings APIs focus on text only.

faq.q: What's the typical latency of an embeddings API call?
faq.a: Single-item calls typically return in 100–500ms depending on the provider and network. Batch calls are much more efficient: embedding 1,000 items often takes 2–5 seconds. This is why batching is standard practice.

title: gRPC (LLM Serving)
tldr: HTTP/2 RPC framework using protobuf for low-latency, schema-enforced internal LLM serving.
plain_english: gRPC is like a private, efficient postal system for servers talking to each other—faster and stricter than the standard HTTP+JSON approach. Instead of sending text messages (JSON), you send compact binary packets (protobuf), and both sides must agree on the exact structure upfront. It's overkill for public-facing APIs, but essential when your inference servers need to handle thousands of requests per second without wasting bandwidth.
how_it_works: You define message schemas and service methods in a `.proto` file. gRPC compiles this into client and server code that uses HTTP/2 (not HTTP/1.1), enabling bidirectional streaming and multiplexing. The client sends a binary-encoded request; the server processes it and returns a binary response. Both sides serialize/deserialize using protobuf, which is faster and smaller than JSON. Triton Inference Server and NVIDIA NIM expose gRPC endpoints for this reason—internal services can stream token outputs or batch inference requests without the overhead of parsing JSON on every round trip.
why_it_matters: At scale, JSON serialization and HTTP/1.1 request overhead add latency and consume bandwidth. gRPC cuts both. Schema enforcement via protobuf catches client–server mismatches at compile time, not runtime. For internal LLM platforms handling 1000+ QPS, the difference between gRPC and REST is often 20–40% latency reduction.
example: A Kubernetes cluster runs ten inference pods serving Llama 2. The orchestrator uses gRPC to stream token predictions from each pod to the gateway, which collects results and batches them for the next layer. Each pod can handle 500 QPS over gRPC; over JSON-REST, the same pod bottlenecks at 300 QPS due to serialization and framing overhead.
pitfalls: gRPC requires code generation and language-specific stubs; adding a new client language (Rust, Go, Python) means building and maintaining those bindings.
Debugging gRPC traffic requires protobuf-aware tools (grpcurl, ghz); standard curl and browser developer tools are useless.
HTTP/2 requires TLS in most deployments, adding setup complexity; self-signed certs in dev can trip up teams unfamiliar with mTLS.
If your clients are web browsers or simple curl scripts, gRPC-Web is a separate layer that adds latency and complexity.
when_use: Use gRPC when you control both client and server code, need sub-50ms latency, expect >100 concurrent connections, or require bidirectional streaming between internal services.
when_avoid: Avoid gRPC for public APIs, browser-based frontends, simple REST microservices, or teams without DevOps/infrastructure experience.

faq.q: Is gRPC the same as protobuf?
faq.a: No. Protobuf is the serialization format (how data is encoded). gRPC is the RPC framework that uses protobuf. You can use protobuf without gRPC, but gRPC typically pairs with protobuf for efficiency.

faq.q: Can I use gRPC for a public API?
faq.a: Technically yes, but it's awkward. Browsers cannot call gRPC directly; you'd need gRPC-Web, which adds a proxy layer. For public APIs, HTTP+JSON is simpler and more accessible.

faq.q: How do I debug gRPC calls?
faq.a: Use grpcurl for command-line requests, or tools like Kreya or Postman (limited support). For detailed traffic inspection, enable gRPC logging and use Wireshark with protobuf dissectors. Standard curl won't work.

faq.q: Does gRPC require TLS?
faq.a: Not strictly, but HTTP/2 (which gRPC uses) works best over TLS in production. Many libraries and proxies default to enforcing it, so plan for mTLS in any real deployment.

faq.q: What's faster: gRPC or REST JSON?
faq.a: gRPC is typically 20–40% faster due to binary serialization and HTTP/2 multiplexing. The gap widens at high QPS and payload sizes; REST wins on simplicity for low-traffic scenarios.

title: JSON Schema Enforcement
tldr: Provider-side guarantee that LLM output validates against a JSON schema at decoding time.
plain_english: Instead of hoping the model produces valid JSON and retrying when it fails, you give the provider a schema upfront and they enforce it during generation. The model can only output tokens that produce valid JSON matching your schema—keys, types, everything. Think of it like a guard at the exit checking every ticket before letting anyone through.
how_it_works: The LLM provider uses constrained decoding to mask invalid tokens at each generation step. When you call the API with `response_format: {type: json_schema, schema: {...}}` (OpenAI) or equivalent (Anthropic, others), the schema is parsed and only tokens that keep the output on a valid path are allowed. The model cannot deviate—if your schema requires `name` as a string and `age` as an integer, those constraints are enforced at the token level, not post-hoc. Output is guaranteed valid without retry loops.
why_it_matters: Eliminates the failure mode where you parse JSON and hit `KeyError` or type mismatches. Downstream systems can trust the structure immediately and skip validation code. This saves latency (no retries), reduces error handling overhead, and makes integration with type-safe systems straightforward.
example: A customer support team uses an LLM to extract ticket metadata. They define a schema requiring `{"severity": "low"|"medium"|"high", "category": string, "needs_escalation": boolean}`. With enforcement, every response is valid JSON matching those types—no null surprises, no string-instead-of-boolean. They skip writing retry logic and feed results directly into their ticketing system.
pitfalls: Schemas that are too restrictive can cause the model to refuse valid requests or produce repetitive output—test your enum values against real data.
Not all providers support the same schema subset; OpenAI's implementation differs from Anthropic's, so migration requires schema adjustments.
The model still hallucinates within the schema—enforcement ensures valid structure, not semantic correctness.
Complex nested schemas with many optional fields can cause token overhead during decoding, slightly increasing latency.
when_use: Use this when you need guaranteed structured output for downstream type-safe systems, extraction tasks with fixed fields, or workflows where retry loops are unacceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid when your output structure is highly variable, when the model needs creative freedom, or when you're targeting providers that don't support constrained decoding.

faq.q: Is JSON schema enforcement the same as function calling?
faq.a: No. Function calling lets you specify discrete functions the model can invoke; schema enforcement constrains the raw output format. Function calling is for tool selection; schema enforcement is for structure guarantee.

faq.q: Does schema enforcement slow down generation?
faq.a: Slightly. Constrained decoding adds a small overhead per token to check validity, but you save latency by eliminating retries. Simple schemas have minimal impact; very complex ones are more expensive.

faq.q: Can I use JSON schema enforcement with streaming?
faq.a: Yes, most providers support streaming with schemas. The constraint is applied token-by-token, so output is streamed incrementally while remaining valid.

faq.q: What happens if the schema is impossible to satisfy?
faq.a: The model will generate partial output matching the schema constraints, then stop. Some providers error; others truncate. Test edge cases before production.

title: LiteLLM
tldr: Open-source Python SDK that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind a single OpenAI-compatible API.
plain_english: LiteLLM is a translation layer for AI models. Instead of writing separate code for OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Mistral, you write once and swap providers by changing a string. It's like adapting a recipe to work in any kitchen—same steps, different appliances.
how_it_works: LiteLLM intercepts your OpenAI-style API call (model, messages, temperature, etc.) and converts it to the correct provider's format before sending it. When you call `completion()`, LiteLLM maps the request to Claude, GPT, or Mistral using the `model` parameter as a routing key. The response normalizes back to OpenAI format. LiteLLM Proxy mode runs as a server, adding cost tracking, key management, budget enforcement, and observability middleware between your app and providers.
why_it_matters: Provider lock-in costs time and risk. Switching models mid-project means rewriting integrations. LiteLLM lets you swap from GPT-4 to Claude to a local Ollama instance without touching your application code, making cost optimization and disaster recovery automatic decisions rather than rewrites.
example: A startup using GPT-4 for customer support hits rate limits and costs spike. Instead of refactoring, they change one config line to route 30% of traffic to Claude and 20% to Mistral via LiteLLM, cutting costs 40% while testing alternative models. When a provider goes down, they failover automatically.
pitfalls: Assuming all providers return identical behavior—latency, output quality, and reasoning depth vary; test each provider in staging.
Forgetting provider-specific limits—Claude has 200k context, GPT-4o has 128k; routing long documents to the wrong model fails silently.
Over-relying on Proxy mode without monitoring—cost tracking and budgets prevent surprises, but misconfigured rate limits block all traffic.
Mixing old and new OpenAI client versions—LiteLLM compatibility breaks if your codebase uses deprecated SDK patterns.
when_use: Use LiteLLM when you support multiple providers, need cost optimization across models, want to A/B test provider performance, or require fallback routing during outages.
when_avoid: Avoid LiteLLM if you're locked into a single proprietary API (Bloomberg terminal) or need sub-millisecond latency where translation overhead matters.

faq.q: Can I use LiteLLM with Ollama or local models?
faq.a: Yes. LiteLLM wraps Ollama, vLLM, and LM Studio the same way it wraps OpenAI—you point it to your local server URL and reference the model by name.

faq.q: Does LiteLLM add latency?
faq.a: Library mode adds 5–15ms overhead (request parsing and routing). Proxy mode adds 10–30ms. For most applications, this is negligible versus network round-trip time.

faq.q: Can LiteLLM track spending across providers?
faq.a: Yes. LiteLLM Proxy logs input/output tokens per provider and calculates costs in real time. You can set per-user or per-model budgets that block requests when limits are hit.

faq.q: What happens if a provider is down?
faq.a: In library mode, the call fails. In Proxy mode, you can configure fallback routing—if OpenAI fails, automatically retry with Anthropic.

faq.q: Is LiteLLM compatible with LangChain or LlamaIndex?
faq.a: Yes. Both frameworks support custom LLM providers that accept OpenAI-format calls, so LiteLLM works as a drop-in replacement.

title: LLM Gateway
tldr: Middleware that routes requests across LLM providers, handles retries, caches responses, and tracks costs centrally.
plain_english: An LLM gateway sits between your application and the actual LLM providers—think of it as a traffic controller and accountant combined. Instead of your app talking directly to OpenAI, Anthropic, or Mistral, it talks to the gateway. The gateway forwards requests intelligently, retries failed calls, caches duplicate questions, and logs everything for billing and compliance.
how_it_works: The gateway accepts API calls in a standard format (like OpenAI's chat-completions-api). It parses the request, optionally checks a semantic-caching layer to avoid redundant calls, then routes to the configured provider—or to a fallback if the primary is down. After the provider responds, the gateway logs the call (cost, tokens, latency), applies PII-redaction if configured, and returns the result. Retries happen automatically on rate-limits or transient errors, and you get observability across all models and teams from a single dashboard.
why_it_matters: Direct provider keys scattered across your infrastructure fragment cost tracking, resilience, and compliance. A gateway centralizes these concerns: one place to audit LLM spend, one place to enforce rate-limits, one place to rotate credentials. For teams using multiple providers or serving multiple internal customers, this prevents cost surprises and operational chaos.
example: A startup using Claude for customer support and GPT-4 for code review runs both through a gateway. When Anthropic has an outage, the gateway automatically falls back to a different Claude endpoint or Mistral. The finance team sees exact token costs per feature; the security team sees PII was never logged. Without the gateway, they'd have separate monitoring per provider and no unified fallback.
pitfalls: Adding a gateway introduces network latency; measure time-to-first-token before and after to ensure acceptable impact.
Semantic caching trades freshness for cost savings; use with caution for time-sensitive queries like live news summaries.
Gateway logs can become a compliance liability if not encrypted and retention-limited; treat cached responses as sensitive data.
Fallback chains mask provider issues; monitor which fallbacks are triggered frequently to catch real problems early.
when_use: Use a gateway when you're multi-homing LLMs, need cost visibility across teams, require automatic failover, or must enforce security policies like PII-redaction at scale.
when_avoid: Avoid a gateway for single-provider, latency-critical workloads where sub-10ms overhead is unacceptable, or for one-off prototypes where the operational overhead outweighs the benefit.

faq.q: What's the difference between an LLM gateway and a load balancer?
faq.a: A load balancer distributes traffic across identical backend servers by health and latency. An LLM gateway does that plus provider abstraction, semantic caching, cost accounting, and cross-provider fallback. A gateway is application-aware; a load balancer is agnostic.

faq.q: Does an LLM gateway add latency?
faq.a: Yes, typically 10–50ms for routing, caching checks, and logging. For latency-critical use cases, benchmark your specific gateway and payload size. Semantic caching can recover latency by avoiding downstream calls entirely.

faq.q: Can I use a gateway with local models?
faq.a: Yes. Gateways like LiteLLM and Portkey support local inference servers (Ollama, vLLM, llama-cpp-server) alongside cloud providers. This lets you fall back to local models if cloud APIs fail or become expensive.

faq.q: Will a gateway lock me into a specific provider?
faq.a: No—that's the point. A gateway abstracts away provider-specific APIs. You can swap Claude for Mixtral or GPT-4 by changing a config file, not rewriting application code.

faq.q: How much does adding a gateway cost?
faq.a: Gateways charge per-million tokens or flat monthly (typically $50–500). The ROI comes from semantic-cache hits reducing redundant calls, plus reduced emergency incident costs from automatic failover.

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
tldr: Open standard for AI assistants to connect to external tools and data sources via a single, reusable server interface.
plain_english: Instead of building custom integrations between each AI app and each external service, MCP lets you build one server that speaks a standard language. Any MCP-compatible client—Claude, Cursor, or your own app—can then talk to that server to access tools, databases, or APIs. Think of it like a universal adapter: your server is the plug, the protocol is the standard socket, and any MCP client can connect.
how_it_works: You create an MCP server that wraps access to a resource (GitHub, Notion, a database). The server exposes tools, resources, and prompts via a JSON-RPC interface over stdio or HTTP. An MCP client (like Claude) sends a request following the protocol spec, the server processes it, and returns structured JSON. The client then uses the result in its reasoning or passes it to the user. You define what the server does; the protocol handles how client and server talk.
why_it_matters: MCP is becoming the standard for AI tool integration, reducing duplication and letting teams focus on server logic rather than per-client integration code. If you build for multiple AI platforms or clients, adopting MCP early saves engineering time and makes your service work with future clients automatically.
example: A data team builds an MCP server wrapping their company's Postgres database with parameterized queries for sales, inventory, and customer data. They deploy it once. Claude, Cursor, and their internal Slack bot can all query it without custom code. When they add a new AI client later, it works immediately because it already speaks MCP.
pitfalls: Treating MCP as a replacement for function calling or tool use—it's a protocol layer on top, not an alternative way to invoke functions.
Building a server that exposes too many unguarded operations; always validate and scope permissions on the server side.
Assuming all MCP clients implement the spec identically; test your server against multiple clients to catch spec interpretation gaps.
Forgetting that MCP is stateless per request; maintain session or auth context in your server, not in the protocol messages.
when_use: Use MCP when you're building a service, tool, or resource that multiple AI applications or clients need to access, or when you want to share integrations across a team without rewriting them for each model.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP if you're building a single-use integration between one AI client and one backend; direct API calls or function calling will be simpler. Also avoid if your use case requires real-time bidirectional streaming MCP doesn't currently support.

faq.q: Is MCP the same as function calling?
faq.a: No. Function calling is how a model invokes a tool; MCP is a protocol for how a client (which includes models) discovers and communicates with external servers. MCP can transport function calls, but it's a layer above them.

faq.q: Can I use MCP with models other than Claude?
faq.a: Yes. MCP is model-agnostic. Any AI application or agent framework that implements the MCP client spec can use your MCP server. Adoption is growing across Cursor, Zed, and other tools.

faq.q: Do I need to host an MCP server?
faq.a: You can run it locally, on a server you control, or via a hosted service. The client connects via stdio or HTTP—deployment is up to you. Many teams run MCP servers locally or on internal infrastructure for data access.

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP and REST APIs?
faq.a: REST is a general web architecture; MCP is a specific protocol optimized for AI clients to discover tools, prompts, and resources in a standardized format. MCP is simpler for AI use cases because it bakes in context awareness.

faq.q: Does MCP handle authentication?
faq.a: MCP itself doesn't define auth; that's your server's responsibility. You control auth inside your server logic—use API keys, OAuth, or whatever suits your architecture.

title: MCP Client
tldr: An application that connects to MCP servers and exposes their tools, prompts, and resources to an LLM.
plain_english: An MCP client is a host application—like Claude Desktop or your custom agent—that discovers and connects to MCP servers, then translates what the LLM wants to do (call a tool, read a resource) into server requests. Think of it as a router: the LLM generates intent, the client figures out which server handles it and sends the request there.
how_it_works: The client reads a configuration file listing available MCP servers and their connection details. It establishes connections and queries each server for its capabilities (tools, resources, prompts). When the LLM decides to call a tool, the client routes that request to the correct server, waits for the result, and returns it to the LLM. The client also handles permission prompts, error recovery, and multiplexing requests across many simultaneous servers without blocking.
why_it_matters: Understanding the client/server boundary is essential for building agents that actually work. A client lets you compose heterogeneous services (your internal APIs, third-party data sources, file systems) into a single coherent tool ecosystem. Custom clients unlock bespoke workflows—e.g., a researcher client that auto-routes between academic databases, code clients, and domain-specific servers.
example: A data analyst uses Claude Desktop (the MCP client) configured with three servers: one connecting to PostgreSQL, one to a file browser on their local drive, and one to Slack. When the LLM needs to query the database, the client forwards the SQL to the PostgreSQL server; when it needs to read a CSV, the file server handles it. All three work in the same session without the LLM knowing which server is which.
pitfalls: Assuming the client is a transparent pass-through: clients must handle authentication, retry logic, and capability negotiation; misconfiguration breaks the whole chain.
Mixing server discovery with client logic: hardcoding server locations in your client code makes scaling and testing painful; use configuration files instead.
Ignoring permission and error boundaries: clients should prompt users before exposing sensitive server capabilities and gracefully degrade if a server goes down.
when_use: Use an MCP client when you need to wire multiple tool sources (APIs, databases, file systems, custom services) into an LLM-driven workflow and want them to feel like a unified interface.
when_avoid: Avoid building a custom client if you're testing a single-server integration; use the existing Claude Desktop or Cursor client first. Don't build a client if your workflow only needs one static tool endpoint.

faq.q: What's the difference between an MCP client and an MCP server?
faq.a: A server exposes capabilities (tools, resources, prompts) and waits for requests. A client discovers servers, maintains connections, and routes LLM requests to the right server. Clients are consumers; servers are providers.

faq.q: Can I run multiple MCP servers with one client?
faq.a: Yes. That's the main point. A single client can discover and connect to many servers simultaneously, and the LLM can call tools from any of them in the same session.

faq.q: Do I need to write code to build an MCP client?
faq.a: Not always. Claude Desktop and Cursor are production MCP clients; you configure them via JSON. You only write code if you need a custom client for a specific application or use case.

faq.q: How does an MCP client handle server failures?
faq.a: That depends on the client implementation. Well-designed clients implement timeout limits, retry logic, and graceful degradation so a failed server doesn't crash the entire LLM session.

faq.q: Can an MCP client run on my local machine?
faq.a: Yes. Clients can run locally (Claude Desktop, Cursor) or as a cloud service. Local clients connect to servers via stdio, local sockets, or network calls, depending on the server setup.

title: MCP Prompts
tldr: Reusable prompt templates MCP servers expose to clients, usually surfaced as slash-commands or workflow shortcuts.
plain_english: Think of MCP prompts as recipe templates. Instead of writing the same multi-step instructions every time, an MCP server packages them once—e.g., 'summarize-github-pr'—and your client (Claude, an IDE, a bot) offers it as a ready-to-use option. You fill in a parameter (like the PR number), and the server returns a fully-formed prompt ready to send to an LLM.
how_it_works: An MCP server defines prompts by declaring a name, description, and argument schema. When a client connects, it discovers these prompts and typically exposes them in the UI—slash-commands or menu items. Users invoke a prompt with parameters; the server substitutes those values into the template, optionally fetches referenced resources or calls tools, and returns the rendered prompt. The client then sends it to an LLM or uses it directly in a workflow.
why_it_matters: Prompts let server authors ship end-to-end workflows without requiring users to memorize or copy-paste instructions. A well-stocked prompt catalog drastically improves UX—teams spend less time on prompt engineering and more time on results. Prompts also encode domain knowledge (GitHub PR summaries, SQL debugging, code reviews) in a way that's instantly discoverable.
example: A GitHub MCP server defines a 'summarize-pr' prompt that takes a repository and PR number. When you type /summarize-pr in Claude Desktop, the server fetches the PR details via its tools, inserts them into a summary template, and returns a ready-to-send prompt. Claude then uses that prompt to write the summary—no manual copy-paste required.
pitfalls: Prompts that are too generic or too rigid fail to adapt to the user's actual need; test them against real use cases.
Missing parameter documentation forces users to guess what arguments to pass.
Prompts that reference server tools without declaring those tools will fail silently if the client doesn't support them.
Overloading a single prompt with too many optional parameters makes it harder to discover and use correctly.
when_use: Use MCP prompts when you're building a server that ships with common workflows or domain expertise (code analysis, data summarization, debugging), and you want clients to surface these as one-click actions.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP prompts if your workflows are highly user-specific or require real-time customization; prefer letting users write their own prompts or compose tools directly instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between an MCP prompt and a system prompt?
faq.a: A system prompt is a global instruction set sent to every LLM call. An MCP prompt is a reusable template that a server exposes for clients to invoke on-demand, often parameterized by user input.

faq.q: Can an MCP prompt call server tools?
faq.a: Yes. A prompt template can include calls to server tools, so the server can fetch live data (e.g., pull PR details from GitHub) before rendering the final prompt.

faq.q: How does a client discover MCP prompts?
faq.a: When a client connects to an MCP server, the server advertises its prompts via the MCP protocol. The client then lists them in its UI, usually as slash-commands or menu items.

faq.q: Are MCP prompts just static templates?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Prompts can be dynamic—the server can inspect parameters, fetch resources, and conditionally include sections before returning the rendered template to the client.

faq.q: Do I need to use MCP prompts if I'm building an MCP server?
faq.a: No. Prompts are optional. You can build a server with only tools or resources. Prompts are a convenience layer for common workflows.

title: MCP Resources
tldr: Read-only data exposed by MCP servers to LLMs—files, database rows, APIs—identified by URI.
plain_english: Resources are how an MCP server hands context to an LLM without the LLM having to fetch it manually. Think of them as labeled data packages: your server says "here's a file at this URI" or "here's a database row at that URI," and the client can include them in the conversation. Unlike tools, resources never change data—they're pure reads.
how_it_works: An MCP server declares resources with a URI (e.g., file:///workspace/docs/spec.md or postgres://prod/users/id:42), a MIME type (text/markdown, application/json), and optional metadata. The client lists available resources and lets users or agents pick which ones to attach to a session. The server returns the resource content on request. The LLM receives the context without needing to know how to query files or databases directly.
why_it_matters: Resources are RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) without custom plumbing. Instead of building retrieval logic yourself, you expose what the LLM needs as resources and let the MCP protocol handle the wiring. This reduces boilerplate in every tool that talks to your data.
example: A code review tool server exposes resources: git repo files at file:///repo/src/module.py, PR comments at github://pr/123/comments, and test results at postgres://ci_db/runs/latest. An LLM client picks the PR diff and test results, attaches them to the conversation, and the model reviews code with full context—no manual API calls in the LLM chain.
pitfalls: Treating resources as a substitute for tools: resources are read-only context, not actions. Use tools for writes or side effects.
Exposing resources without URI consistency: clients rely on URIs to deduplicate and cache. Inconsistent naming breaks session state.
Overloading a single resource with too much data: large responses block the client. Break them into smaller, scoped resources instead.
Forgetting MIME type or metadata: clients use these to interpret and prioritize resources. Missing them breaks intelligent resource selection.
when_use: Use resources when you need to hand static or slowly-changing context (docs, schema, logs, database snapshots) to an LLM without custom retrieval code.
when_avoid: Avoid resources if the data changes frequently during a session, requires complex filtering logic, or needs write operations. Use tools instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP resources and MCP tools?
faq.a: Resources are read-only context the server exposes. Tools are actions the LLM can call with side effects. Use resources for data, tools for operations.

faq.q: Can an MCP resource be large?
faq.a: Technically yes, but large resources block the client and waste context. Break them into smaller, scoped resources by entity, time range, or file.

faq.q: How do clients decide which resources to include?
faq.a: Clients typically show users a list of available resources and let them select manually, or agents/tools can request specific resources by URI.

faq.q: Do MCP resources support real-time updates?
faq.a: No. Resources are snapshots. If data changes mid-session, the client must re-fetch. For live data, use tools that query on-demand instead.

faq.q: Can I use a resource URI to access a private database?
faq.a: Yes, if your MCP server has database credentials. The server handles auth; the client just sees the URI. But expose only what the LLM should see.

title: MCP Sampling
tldr: MCP protocol feature that lets a server request LLM text generation from the client without managing its own model.
plain_english: Normally your client (like Claude Desktop) talks to an LLM, then calls tools on a server. MCP sampling flips that: the server asks the client to generate text using the LLM it already has access to. Think of it like asking your assistant to use their own reference library instead of requiring you to hand them one. The client typically shows you a permission prompt before responding.
how_it_works: When an MCP server needs generated text, it sends a sampling request to the client with a prompt, temperature, and other generation parameters. The client shows the user a disclosure prompt (so they know a tool is asking the LLM to generate text), then forwards the request to its LLM. The LLM returns completions, which the client sends back to the server. The server gets the result without storing API keys or bundling a model. This works because the MCP protocol includes a sampling sub-interface alongside tools, resources, and prompts.
why_it_matters: Sampling lets MCP servers implement LLM-powered features—summarization, analysis, generation—without needing per-tool API keys, local model infrastructure, or end-to-end model management. It also preserves privacy: a tool can ask the user's trusted client to generate text without the tool's code seeing the LLM directly. This unlocks a new class of MCP patterns: stateless, composable, LLM-backed tools.
example: A code-review MCP server can ask Claude Desktop to summarize a pull request's diff, extract key concerns, and suggest improvements—all through sampling. The server sends the diff as a prompt; Claude generates the summary using its own context window; the server receives the text and formats it into the review. No API keys, no rate limits to manage per-tool, no extra model downloads.
pitfalls: Assuming sampling is automatic: the user must explicitly approve each sampling request in the client UI, so don't rely on sampling for real-time critical paths.

Treating sampling prompts like tool inputs: sampling requests run through the client's full LLM pipeline (including system prompts and temperature), so results vary; design tool logic to handle variance.
Forgetting the user context: sampling runs with the user's LLM and settings, not a neutral baseline, so the output reflects their model choice (Claude vs. GPT vs. local model).
Overloading sampling for tool control: sampling is for text generation, not for routing decisions; if your tool needs deterministic logic, use function calls and conditional branches, not sampling.
when_use: Use MCP sampling when a server tool needs to generate, summarize, analyze, or transform text and the client has trusted LLM access; sampling is ideal for workflows where the LLM sits between the user and the tool.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP sampling when you need guaranteed deterministic output, sub-100ms latency, or the tool requires a specific model or fine-tune; also avoid if the tool must operate offline or the server cannot handle approval delays.

faq.q: Is MCP sampling the same as MCP tools?
faq.a: No. Tools let a server expose functions that the client or LLM can call. Sampling is the reverse: the server asks the client to run the LLM. Tools are for server-side action; sampling is for LLM-side generation.

faq.q: Do I need API keys to use MCP sampling?
faq.a: No. The server doesn't manage keys—the client does. Sampling works because your client is already authenticated to an LLM (Claude, OpenAI, etc.); the server just borrows that connection.

faq.q: Can I use MCP sampling offline?
faq.a: Only if your client has offline LLM access (e.g., a local model). If the client uses a cloud API, sampling requires internet and authentication to that API.

faq.q: What happens if the user denies a sampling request?
faq.a: The client returns an error to the server. The server must handle rejection gracefully—either fall back to a default response, skip the operation, or ask the user differently.

faq.q: Does sampling work with all MCP clients?
faq.a: Sampling is part of the MCP spec, but not all clients implement it. Desktop clients like Claude Desktop do; simpler clients might not. Check your client's documentation.

title: MCP Server
tldr: A program exposing resources, prompts, and tools over the Model Context Protocol for LLM clients to consume.
plain_english: An MCP server is like a plugin that lets AI applications talk to your tools and data. It listens for requests from Claude, Cursor, or other MCP-compatible clients and responds with information or actions. You run it locally or on a server, and it declares what it can do—fetch files, search databases, call APIs—so clients know what to ask.
how_it_works: You implement the MCP server interface in Python, TypeScript, Kotlin, or another supported language. The server connects to a client over stdio, HTTP+SSE, or another transport and registers its capabilities: resources (data sources), prompts (templates), and tools (functions). When a client calls a method, the server processes the request and returns the result. The client stays in control of the conversation; the server only responds when asked.
why_it_matters: MCP servers are the standard way to give AI applications access to your product's APIs, documents, and workflows without building custom integrations for each AI tool. They reduce friction for users adopting Claude, Cursor, or Cline with your platform.
example: A GitHub MCP server registers tools like 'create-issue' and 'search-repos'. When you ask Claude in Cursor to 'open an issue in my frontend repo with these steps', Claude calls the server's create-issue tool, which constructs the GitHub API call and returns the new issue URL. You write the server once; it works with any MCP-compatible client.
pitfalls: Exposing unsafe operations without auth checks—MCP servers run in the user's environment, so validate all inputs and respect permissions.
Not handling streaming resources efficiently—large datasets can block the client; use pagination or chunked responses.
Inconsistent error messages—clients depend on machine-readable error codes to retry or degrade gracefully.
Forgetting to version your schema—breaking changes to tool arguments will confuse clients expecting the old format.
when_use: Use an MCP server when you want to expose your product's data or actions to multiple AI applications (Claude, Cursor, Cline, etc.) without writing a separate integration for each.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP servers if you need real-time bidirectional communication (use WebSockets instead) or if your use case is a one-off integration with a single client (a direct API may be simpler).

faq.q: What's the difference between an MCP server and an MCP client?
faq.a: A server exposes resources and tools; a client (like Claude Desktop) consumes them. The server runs in your environment or on your infrastructure; the client runs on the user's machine or in their app.

faq.q: Do I need to use an official SDK to write an MCP server?
faq.a: No. Official SDKs for Python and TypeScript simplify implementation, but you can implement the protocol directly if you understand the JSON-RPC message format and transport layer.

faq.q: Can an MCP server run in the cloud?
faq.a: Yes. You can deploy it as a containerized service (Docker, Kubernetes) and expose it over HTTP+SSE or gRPC. Clients connect via a remote endpoint instead of stdio.

faq.q: Is an MCP server secure for exposing sensitive APIs?
faq.a: Only if you implement authentication and authorization. An MCP server is a network service, so use API keys, TLS, and permission checks—don't rely on client-side validation.

faq.q: What transport protocols does MCP support?
faq.a: stdio (local), HTTP+SSE (server-sent events), and HTTP with streaming. stdio is simplest for local tools; HTTP variants work for remote servers.

title: MCP Tools
tldr: Functions an MCP server exposes to LLMs, enabling side-effecting actions like running SQL or creating issues.
plain_english: Think of MCP tools as the "do something" actions available through an MCP server. While resources let you read data and prompts give you templates, tools let you perform operations—execute queries, write files, post messages. The LLM decides when to call them based on what you're asking it to do.
how_it_works: An MCP server defines tools as JSON Schema objects describing inputs, outputs, and behavior. The client exposes these to the LLM, which can invoke them via tool-use messages. When the tool runs, it sends back structured results (success, error, or data). The LLM sees the outcome and decides next steps—retry, call another tool, or respond to you.
why_it_matters: Tools are how agents interact with external systems. Without them, LLMs can only read and suggest; with them, they can act. Most MCP adoption centers on defining and consuming the right tools for your workflow.
example: A code review bot uses MCP tools to fetch pull requests from GitHub (tool: get_pr), run linters against the diff (tool: lint_code), and post comments back (tool: post_comment). Each tool is described with JSON Schema so the LLM knows what parameters to pass and what to expect back.
pitfalls: Exposing a tool with vague input schema forces the LLM to guess parameters—always write precise JSON Schema with required fields and descriptions.
Tools that take too long to execute (>10s) will timeout in many LLM integrations; break expensive operations into smaller, faster tools.
Returning raw errors without context leaves the LLM unable to recover; wrap tool results with suggested next steps on failure.
Defining too many tools (>20) causes the LLM to forget which ones exist or call them incorrectly; group related operations and keep the set lean.
when_use: Use MCP tools when you need an LLM to perform actions—write data, call APIs, run commands, or modify external systems.
when_avoid: Avoid tools for read-only access (use MCP resources instead) or for reusable instruction templates (use MCP prompts).

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP tools and function calling?
faq.a: Function calling is an LLM capability; MCP tools are the protocol layer for exposing functions over MCP. An MCP server uses function calling under the hood to let clients define and invoke tools.

faq.q: Can an MCP tool return multiple results?
faq.a: Yes. A tool can return structured content with multiple fields or arrays. The LLM sees all of it and can decide what to do next based on the full result.

faq.q: How does the MCP server know if a tool call succeeded?
faq.a: The tool handler in the server returns a result object. You control the structure—include a status field, error message, or data. The client passes this back to the LLM so it can react.

faq.q: What happens if a tool takes too long to run?
faq.a: Most LLM integrations have a timeout (typically 30–60 seconds). If your tool exceeds it, the call fails. Design tools to be fast, or split long operations into separate tools that report progress.

faq.q: Can I make a tool optional—only available in some MCP clients?
faq.a: Yes. Each tool definition is independent. A client only sees and can call tools that the server declares. You can conditionally register tools based on configuration or capabilities.
